claims.claim: Thermal runaway is defined as a phenomenon that occurs when the heat generation rate exceeds 1 °C/min.
claims.quote: thermal runaway, which can be defined as a phenomenon that occurs when the heat generation rate exceeds 1 ◦C min–1.

claims.claim: The DSC measurement of the separator showed two endothermic reactions at approximately 125°C and 155°C, corresponding to pore-closing and thermal-shrinkage deformation, respectively.
claims.quote: two endothermic reactions occurring at around 125 ◦C (the first reaction indicated by (i) in the figure) and 155 ◦C (the second reaction indicated by (ii) in the figure) could be observed. Based on the DSC results... the first and second endothermic reactions depicted in Fig. 1(a) were considered to be a pore-closing reaction and thermal-shrinkage deformation, respectively.

claims.claim: At approximately 125°C, a rapid increase in internal resistance by almost two orders of magnitude and a reduction in OCV by a few mV were observed, caused by separator shutdown.
claims.quote: A rapid increase in the internal resistance by almost two orders of magnitude and a reduction in the OCV by a few mV were observed at point A (approximately 125 ◦C). Based the first endothermic reaction of DSC in Fig. 1(a), this rapid change in the internal resistance was potentialy caused by a separator shutdown, which completely closed the pore.

claims.claim: Shrinkage of the separator induced an internal short circuit, which triggered Joule heat, resulting in self-heating observed at 175°C in the ARC test.
claims.quote: the shrinkage of the separator induced an internal short circuit, which was further considered to trigger Joule heat, thereby resulting in the self-heating observed at 175 ◦C.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway in the LFP battery required a temperature above 220°C and could not occur from Joule heat alone; additional heating was necessary.
claims.quote: thermal runaway did not occur owing to this process alone, as thermal runaway requires a temperature above 220 ◦C that necessitates additional heating.

claims.claim: For NCA 18650 cells, overcharged conditions at 132% and 143% SoC showed onset temperatures of thermal runaway as low as 80°C and 65°C, respectively, attributed to lithium deposition on the anode.
claims.quote: For NCA cell, the overcharged conditions showed onset temperatures as low as 80°C and 65°C for 132% and 143% SoC levels, respectively. The reason for this substantial drop in onset temperature in case of the overcharged cells was attributed to the lithium deposition on the anode which compromised the thermal stability.

claims.claim: Aged Li-ion cells (70% SoH, NCM/graphite 18650) showed onset of exothermic reactions shifted approximately 30°C lower than fresh cells, and unlike fresh cells, aged cells did not show any variation with SoC level.
claims.quote: the OER for the aged cells showed a strong shift of temperature to nearly 30°C (red curves in Fig. 15), which marked the lowest onset temperature of all the experiments. The reason for this shift was attributed to the high surface area lithium (HSAL) metal deposition on the anode surface of the aged cells... Moreover, unlike fresh cells, aged cells did not show any variation with the SoC level.

claims.claim: The fully discharged LCO Li-ion cell (0% SoC) did not show any sign of thermal runaway, while cells with SoC levels above 50% showed substantially lower onset temperatures and exponentially increasing self-heating rates.
claims.quote: the fully discharged cell (0% SoC) did not show any sign of thermal runaway which clearly explained the severity of thermal effects of a battery under operation... the SoC level of more than 50% has been observed to have substantial impact on thermal instability and hence on thermal runaway behavior of the battery.

claims.claim: Under one-sided heating of a prismatic 50 Ah LMO cell, the critical temperature for thermal runaway was substantially delayed to 281°C due to the large temperature gradient in the cell, compared to 192°C for both-sided heating with internal short circuit.
claims.quote: Due to the large temperature gradient in the cell, the thermal runaway was substantially delayed, and the critical temperature was observed at 281°C.

claims.claim: Mini-channel cooling fails to prevent thermal runaway in a nail-penetrated (short-circuited) cell even at high coolant flow rates, but can effectively prevent heat propagation from the short-circuited cell to neighboring cells at adequate flow rates.
claims.quote: the mini-channel cooling fails to prevent the thermal runaway in the short-circuited cell even by employing high flow rates of the coolant through the channels. However, as depicted in Fig. 36c, propagation of heat from one cell to the neighboring cells could be efficiently prevented by employing adequate flow rate of the coolant

claims.claim: Before the battery temperature approaches the uncontrollable temperature, the gas release of LFP, LMO, and NCM batteries during stage B is 0.094 mol, 0.042 mol, and 0.058 mol, respectively.
claims.quote: the gas released from the LFP battery is 0.094 mol, the gas released from the LMO battery is 0.042 mol, and the gas released from the NCM battery is 0.058 mol.

claims.claim: The gas released before safety venting accounts for 29.1%, 75.2%, and 55.4% of the total gas causing the safety valve to open in LFP, LMO, and NCM batteries, respectively.
claims.quote: the gas release which causes the safety venting, accounts for 29.1, 75.2 and 55.4% of the total gas in LFP, LMO and NCM batteries, respectively.

claims.claim: Safety venting of the LFP battery is mainly due to electrolyte volatilization, whereas safety venting of LMO and NCM batteries is mainly caused by gas release.
claims.quote: the safety venting of the LFP battery is mainly due to the volatilization of the electrolyte. However, the safety venting of the LMO and NCM batteries is mainly caused by the gas release.

claims.claim: The NCM battery has the greatest total pressure change during thermal runaway at 9.817 MPa, which is 4.3 times the pressure change of the LFP battery.
claims.quote: The NCM battery has the greatest pressure change, P = 9.817 MPa, which is 4.3 times the pressure change of the LFP battery.

claims.claim: For the LFP battery after thermal runaway, gas release is the main cause of structural change in the cathode material, whereas for LMO and NCM batteries, impact force is the dominant cause of cathode material structural change.
claims.quote: For the LFP battery, gas release is the main source, and for LMO and NCM batteries, the impact force is the dominant cause.
